title: Variables and expressions
boardSubtitle: Aim with fixed inputs first, then use an assignment to store and reuse the portal side.
unlockSpotlightText:
Variables let you store values under names you choose. This can make code easier to read.

New: 'choose_input(...)' - choose which input the ball launches from.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What a variable is
unlockPrimerCards.body: A variable is a named place in the program where you can store a value and use it again later.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What assignment does
unlockPrimerCards.body: 'variable_name = 3' is an assignment statement. It stores the value 3 in a variable called 'variable_name'.

unlockPrimerCards.title: Use the stored value
unlockPrimerCards.body: To aim the ball, you can write 'choose_input(3)', which sets the input to 3. After that, dropped balls come from that input. You can also write 'input = 3' and then 'choose_input(input)'.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 1: Drop a ball from input 1 (left)
practiceGoals.instruction:
Change the main program.
Add the line 'choose_input(1)' before 'drop_ball()' and run the program.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
Use 'choose_input(number)' to set the input.
The number must be between 1 and 3.
If 'choose_input(number)' comes after 'drop_ball()', it only affects the next ball.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 2: Drop a ball from input 3 (right)
practiceGoals.boardHint: The code can stay the same. You only need to change the number inside 'choose_input(...)'.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 3: Drop the ball toward the portal
practiceGoals.instruction:
Now use the value 'portal_side' inside 'choose_input(...)' instead of a fixed number. You can write 'choose_input(portal_side)' directly, or save it into your own variable first, for example 'target = portal_side'.

* New: portal - when a ball enters it, several new balls appear.
* New: board state - under the pachinko board you can see game-defined variables that may help you.
* New: 'portal_side' variable - its value is the input number currently under the portal.
practiceGoals.boardHint: This goal is about using the value 'portal_side'. You can use it directly in 'choose_input(portal_side)' or save it into your own variable first.

title: Conditionals
boardSubtitle: Read 'next_ball' before the launch and choose the right response.
unlockSpotlightText:
Instead of regular balls, the game now has balls with special properties.

There are 3 ball types:
🔴 Negative ball (negative_ball): you should avoid this ball because it gives negative points.
🟢 Center ball (center_ball): this ball gives bonus points if you drop it from input 2.
🔵 Portal ball (portal_ball): this ball has a stronger portal effect, so launch it toward the portal.

* New: Board state - the variable 'next_ball' was added. It shows which ball will drop next.
* New: 'skip_ball()' - this command skips the next ball and updates the ball queue.
* New: conditionals ('if', 'elif', and 'else') - these let you react to different situations.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What 'if', 'elif', and 'else' do
unlockPrimerCards.body: 'if' checks the first condition. 'elif' lets you check another condition if the first one was false, and 'else' handles everything left over.

unlockPrimerCards.title: Your routing plan
unlockPrimerCards.body:
Use the value 'next_ball' to decide which ball is coming next.

1. Skip 'negative_ball'
2. Send 'portal_ball' to the portal
3. Send 'center_ball' to input 2

practiceGoals.title: Goal 1: Skip 'negative_ball'
practiceGoals.instruction:
The next ball is 'negative_ball'.
Write an 'if' statement that reads 'next_ball' and skips this ball with 'skip_ball()'.
Do not drop any balls.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
An 'if' statement runs its body when the condition is true. For example:
if 1 == 1:
    drop_ball()

This drops a ball because '1 == 1' is true. Use '==' for comparison, not '='.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 2: Send the remaining balls toward the portal
practiceGoals.instruction:
The next ball is 'portal_ball'.
Keep the negative-ball 'if' statement and add an 'else' branch.
In the 'else' branch, drop the ball toward the portal.
practiceGoals.boardHint: 'else' runs when the previous 'if' in the same chain was not true. Use a board value for the portal direction, not a fixed number.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 3: Launch 'center_ball' from input 2
practiceGoals.instruction:
The next ball is 'center_ball'.
Write a program that drops this ball from input 2.
practiceGoals.boardHint: 'center_ball' gives a bonus when you choose the center input before dropping the ball.

title: Functions
boardSubtitle: Store one reusable tactic in the helper editor and call it from the main program.
unlockSpotlightText:
A function is a named block of code that can be run again later. In this topic, you write one helper function and call it from the main program.

* New: helper editor - write one helper function here.
* New: 'def' - starts a function definition, for example 'def follow_portal():'.
* New: function call - run the helper from the main program by writing its name, for example 'follow_portal()'.

unlockPrimerCards.title: 1. Define the function
unlockPrimerCards.body: A function starts with 'def name():'. The indented lines below it are the function body.

unlockPrimerCards.title: 2. Call the function
unlockPrimerCards.body: In the main program, run the function by writing its name. The call runs every line inside the function body.

unlockPrimerCards.title: 3. Reuse the tactic
unlockPrimerCards.body: Put repeated behavior in the helper function. Then you can use the same tactic without rewriting the same lines.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 1: Call the helper
practiceGoals.instruction:
One function is defined in the helper editor.
Read it and try to understand what the code does. Then call that function from the main program.
practiceGoals.boardHint: To run a function, write its name with parentheses in the main program. Do not copy the helper function body into the main program.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 2: Reuse the helper
practiceGoals.instruction:
The portal moved to the other side.
Call the same helper function again and see whether it still works.
practiceGoals.boardHint: If the helper reads 'portal_side' when it runs, the main program does not need to know where the portal is.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 3: Write 'skip_negative()'
practiceGoals.instruction:
Edit the function in the helper editor.
Put 'def skip_negative():' on the first line.
Change the function body so it checks whether the next ball is 'negative_ball', and if it is, skips the ball.
Call this new function from the main program.
You do not need to drop any balls in this task.
practiceGoals.boardHint: The function needs one 'if' branch. In the condition, read the next ball value, and inside the branch use the command that skips a ball.

title: Loops
boardSubtitle: Use a loop to automate a short preview response.
unlockSpotlightText:
A loop repeats the code inside it several times. This lets one run handle multiple balls in a row.

* New: 'for ... in range(...)' - repeats the indented code a set number of times.
* New: auto-run - run the program repeatedly without pressing the button every time.
* New: 'continue' - ends the current loop step and moves to the next repetition.

unlockPrimerCards.title: 1. Start the loop
unlockPrimerCards.body: 'for' creates a repetition. 'range(3)' means the loop body runs three times.

unlockPrimerCards.title: 2. Add the loop body
unlockPrimerCards.body: The loop body is the indented lines under the loop. Those lines run again on each repetition.

unlockPrimerCards.title: 3. End a step early
unlockPrimerCards.body: When you skip a ball, 'continue' helps move straight to the next repetition.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 1: Repeat the helper 3 times
practiceGoals.instruction:
In the main program, write a loop that calls 'follow_portal()' 3 times.
One run should drop at least 3 balls.
practiceGoals.boardHint: 'range(3)' gives three repetitions. The line inside the loop must be indented.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 2: Repeat the helper 4 times
practiceGoals.instruction:
In this goal, change the number of loop repetitions. One run should drop at least 4 balls.

* New: auto-run - use the auto-run button to run the program repeatedly.
practiceGoals.boardHint: In the previous goal, 'range(3)' gave three repetitions. For four repetitions, change only that number.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 3: Handle 3 balls with a loop
practiceGoals.instruction:
Write a loop that handles the next 3 balls.

On each repetition, check 'next_ball':
* 'portal_ball' - call 'follow_portal()'
* 'negative_ball' - skip the ball
* 'center_ball' - drop the ball from input 2
practiceGoals.boardHint: An 'if / elif / else' chain works well inside the loop. If you skip a ball, use 'continue' after 'skip_ball()' so the current repetition ends immediately and does not move on to later commands in the same loop step.

title: Lists
boardSubtitle: Read the 3 input multipliers, find the strongest one, and farm that input.
unlockSpotlightText:
Each input now has its own multiplier. The higher the multiplier, the more points a ball dropped from that input gives. For a negative ball, the multiplier also makes the penalty stronger.

* New: 'bonus_map' - a list that stores the multipliers for inputs 1, 2, and 3.
* New: indexes - 'bonus_map[0]' is input 1, 'bonus_map[1]' is input 2, and 'bonus_map[2]' is input 3.
* New: best-input search - the multipliers change every run, so it is worth finding the highest multiplier and using that input.

unlockPrimerCards.title: 1. Read the list
unlockPrimerCards.body: 'bonus_map' stores the three input multipliers in the same order as the inputs on the board.

unlockPrimerCards.title: 2. Use an index
unlockPrimerCards.body: List indexes start from zero. That is why 'bonus_map[2]' matches input 3.

unlockPrimerCards.title: 3. Find the highest value
unlockPrimerCards.body: With a loop, you can inspect the multipliers and remember which input has the highest multiplier so far.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 1: Compare two multipliers
practiceGoals.instruction:
Compare the multipliers in 'bonus_map[0]' and 'bonus_map[2]'.
Store the index of the higher multiplier in the variable 'best_index' and then launch one ball from the matching input.
For the comparison you can use 'if bonus_map[0] < bonus_map[2]:'
practiceGoals.boardHint:
Start by assuming the best index is 0. Then check whether 'bonus_map[2]' is greater than 'bonus_map[0]'. If it is, set 'best_index = 2'.

'choose_input(...)' needs an input number from 1-3, so use 'best_index + 1' at the end.
practiceGoals.suggestedSnippet:
best_index = 0
if bonus_map[2] > bonus_map[0]:
    best_index = 2
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()

practiceGoals.title: Goal 2: Find the best multiplier out of all 3
practiceGoals.instruction: Turn the two-slot comparison into a full search. Use a 'for' loop to check all 3 multipliers, keep the highest value and its index, then launch one ball from that input.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
Loop over all of 'bonus_map'. Track the best multiplier so far, the index of that slot, and the current index.

When 'multiplier > best_multiplier', update both 'best_multiplier' and 'best_index'.
practiceGoals.suggestedSnippet:
best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = index
    index = index + 1
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()
practiceGoals.starterProgramSource:
best_index = 0
if bonus_map[2] > bonus_map[0]:
    best_index = 2
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()

practiceGoals.title: Goal 3: Use the best input
practiceGoals.instruction: Use the same best-input search and then drop at least 3 balls from that input with a loop.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
The first part finds the best index just like the previous goal. After the search, add a second loop.

Inside the second loop, call 'choose_input(best_index + 1)' and 'drop_ball()' each time so the same best input runs at least three times.
practiceGoals.suggestedSnippet:
best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = index
    index = index + 1
for shot in range(3):
    choose_input(best_index + 1)
    drop_ball()
practiceGoals.starterProgramSource:
best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = index
    index = index + 1
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()
